---
format:
  docx:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
---

25 June 2026

Dear Editors of *Ecology Letters*,

Please consider our manuscript, "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity", for publication in *Ecology Letters* as a Methods paper.

Extreme heat is rapidly becoming one of the clearest ecological consequences of climate change. Yet a central task remains difficult: translating laboratory heat-tolerance experiments into predictions about which species, populations and biological processes are most at risk under realistic temperature sequences. Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models were developed for exactly this purpose because they link heat intensity, exposure duration and biological failure. However, the field still relies heavily on a two-stage analysis: first estimating a time-to-endpoint at each temperature, then regressing those estimates against temperature. This approach is familiar, but it discards uncertainty, struggles with overdispersed or hierarchical data, weakens group comparisons and makes it hard to carry uncertainty into heat-injury forecasts.

Our paper offers a direct, practical replacement. We derive equivalences showing how four-parameter logistic and related dose-response models recover the classical quantities thermal ecologists already use, including thermal sensitivity ($z$), critical thermal maxima ($CT_{max}$), $T_{crit}$, heat injury and survival, from a single fitted response surface. This is important because it allows the field to keep its existing biological language while replacing a fragile two-stage workflow with a statistically coherent model fit directly to the raw data.

We think the manuscript is especially timely for *Ecology Letters* because the approach changes the ecological inference, not only the statistical machinery. In simulations, the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. In empirical case studies across cereal aphids, snow gum, zebrafish and vinegar flies, the model moves beyond ranking tolerance values to ask sharper ecological questions: which aphid species loses survival under real heat sequences; whether daylight changes the threshold for plant photosystem damage; how oxygen alters the duration-dependence of fish heat tolerance; and which fly vital rates fail before mortality. These are the kinds of inferences needed to predict biological vulnerability during extreme heat.

A second contribution is accessibility. We implement the workflow in two R packages, `bayesTLS` and `freqTLS`, and provide all data, scripts, simulations, an online supplement and a tutorial. The Bayesian implementation makes uncertainty propagation into group contrasts, heat injury and survival prediction straightforward; the frequentist implementation gives users a likelihood-based route to similar point estimates. Together, these tools make the framework usable by experimental thermal biologists, comparative ecologists and climate-impact modellers.

We believe this Methods paper will be of broad interest to the *Ecology Letters* readership because it addresses a general problem in climate-change ecology: how to move from controlled heat experiments to reliable forecasts under natural thermal regimes. It also builds directly on an active, high-profile conversation in this space. Recent work in *Ecology Letters* by Ørsted and colleagues showed that survival, heat coma and reproduction can have different thermal death time relationships and that sublethal failure may precede mortality. Recent work in *PLOS Biology* by Bullard, Buckley and Kingsolver showed that variation in death times can strongly affect predictions of ectotherm responses to stressful temperatures. Our contribution is to provide the general statistical framework and software needed to make these analyses routine, uncertainty-aware and extensible across systems. The framework is not restricted to mortality or to animals. It applies to lethal and sublethal endpoints, count and continuous-proportion data, hierarchical designs, treatment comparisons and, more generally, any dose-response problem where biological response depends jointly on stress intensity and exposure duration. We hope it will help set a new standard for analysing and reporting thermal sensitivity, tolerance and heat-injury prediction.

The manuscript is original, is not under consideration elsewhere and has been approved by all authors.

Thank you for considering our submission.

Sincerely,

Daniel W.A. Noble, Pieter A. Arnold, Shinichi Nakagawa, and Patrice Pottier

![Conceptual overview of the proposed modelling framework.](../output/figs/fig1.png){#fig-cover-framework width=100%}
